iSRL / Digital Public Infrastructure

Guide

Research 101

Lalitha A R  ·  iSRL

A guide for people who want to get started in research and don't know how.

This is not a step 1, step 2 tutorial. It's more of a window into what research actually looks like — at least from where I'm standing. I am not an expert. I'm someone who explored it a bit more than average and is sharing what worked.

Some of what I say will be biased towards systems research. Take what's useful. Leave what isn't.

One more thing before we start: this guide isn't finished. It gets added to when a real question comes up that needs a better answer than "figure it out." If something isn't covered here and you think it should be, email lalithaar.research@gmail.com.

Module i — You and Your Ideas

Before the methods, the citations, the LaTeX tables. This is where you have to start.

i.1 — You Are Worth It

Before anything else.

Nothing that follows in this guide — no feedback, no failed experiment, no rejected preprint, no idea that didn't hold up — is about who you are as a person. Read that again if you need to.

This sounds obvious. It isn't. Most people who struggle in research don't struggle because they lack intelligence or discipline. They struggle because somewhere along the way they tied their sense of worth to whether the idea worked out. When that happens, you stop being honest. You start defending instead of discovering. You can't afford to be wrong, so you quietly avoid the places where you might be. And research lives in exactly those places.

So this comes first, before everything else: you are worth it. Whatever happens to the work.

i.2 — Ideas as Entities

Your ideas are not you.

A paper can be the weakest preprint you ever write, or the most cited work of the year — and either way, it says nothing about who you are. A bad idea does not make you bad. A failed experiment does not make you a failure. These are ideas and outputs. They are separate things from the person producing them.

The way I think about it: try to respect ideas as entities of their own. They pass through you. You are not their owner. You are the person who shows up, does the work, and represents them as faithfully as you can.

This also means: when someone in your team says "this idea doesn't hold up" — they are not talking about you. They are talking about the idea as its own entity. They are not even talking about you as a researcher. They are talking about a specific claim or approach that needs another look.

This distinction is what makes honest collaboration possible. If you hear feedback as a comment on your worth, you will defend. If you hear it as two people looking at an idea together and trying to make it right, you can actually work. One of those produces better research. The other produces a lot of tension and not much else.

i.3 — What Makes Someone a Researcher

It's not a title. It's not a degree. It's a disposition.

The standard academic path — undergrad, then masters, then PhD, then postdoc — exists for real reasons. It builds depth, rigour, access to resources, mentorship structures. If that's the path you're on or considering, it's a valid one. This guide is not a rejection of that.

What this guide is saying is that the disposition that makes someone a researcher — curiosity, willingness to be wrong, ability to sit with a problem that doesn't have an obvious answer — that doesn't require institutional permission to develop. You can build it now, regardless of where you are in the academic structure.

The difference between a programmer and a researcher isn't intelligence. In programming, the problem is defined for you. You know what you're building. Success means the requirements are met and the script runs. In research, you are the one deciding what the problem even is. There's no spec. You define the terms — which also means you're responsible for knowing when you've gone off track, because nothing will throw an error to tell you.

Programming is a tool in research. The same way Grammarly is. The output — the script, the visualisation — is not the end goal. Serving the truth is the end goal. That shift in what you're optimising for is the whole thing.

So: are you ready to start research? If you can sit with a question that has no obvious answer, look for evidence rather than confirmation, and hold your own ideas loosely enough to change them when you find something better — you're already more ready than you think.

Module ii — Truth, Systems, and History

How to relate to the knowledge that came before you — and how to write about it without becoming a judge.

ii.1 — Systems Were Built Within Constraints

When you're doing research, you will find things that look obviously wrong in hindsight. A law from 2011 that didn't account for something that now seems basic. A framework with a gap that seems like it should have been caught. A definition that contradicts a more recent one from the same body.

The instinct is to write about this with a kind of contempt. "The earlier version failed to account for X." "The regulation was inadequate." It's easy to do and it feels like rigour. It usually isn't.

Here's what's almost always actually true: the people who wrote that law in 2011 were trying their best with the data, tools, digital infrastructure, and foresight available to them at that point in time. They weren't being careless or stupid. They were doing what anyone does — working from the constraints they actually had, not the ones you have now looking back with ten more years of evidence.

Systems evolve because reality evolves and feedback accumulates. A 2020 amendment that closes a loophole from 2011 isn't evidence that the 2011 version was badly written. It's evidence that the system is working — that regulators are watching, collecting data, and updating.

When you write about this, your job is to be a scribe, not a judge. You are documenting what existed, what changed, and what the evidence suggests about why. You are not here to deliver a verdict on whether the people involved were good at their jobs.

This matters practically because the language you use signals which one you're doing. There's a difference between:

Loaded wording

"More room for fanciful names and branding; there is a general rule against misleading descriptions, but less structure on the front-of-pack."

Neutral wording

"Provides flexibility for brand-led naming on the front-of-pack, governed by general fair-trading principles rather than prescriptive naming requirements."

Same fact. Completely different register. The first implies something was being gotten away with. The second just says what was there.

A useful test: read your sentence from two sides. If you were the person who wrote the original policy, would this read like a fair account — or like an accusation? If you were someone in the industry it describes, would this feel like a realistic description of your situation — or like you're being painted as someone trying to sidestep the rules? If it's either of those, the wording is carrying a judgment it shouldn't be. What you're aiming for is the boring middle: the kind of sentence that reads like a usual report someone picks up on a usual Tuesday and doesn't think twice about.

ii.2 — You Are a Connector, Not a Genius

Google Scholar has a line on its homepage: Stand on the shoulders of giants.

Take it literally.

You are not expected to produce original insight from nowhere. You are expected to know what has already been said, find where it applies to your question, and build from there. Research is connector work. The genius model — lone person has breakthrough idea — is mostly mythology. What actually happens is: person reads carefully, finds a gap or a contradiction or an underexplored angle, and adds one careful brick.

Think of it this way. You're in a room making a claim. For people to take it seriously, they consider your track record and expertise. Now imagine that for every claim you make, there's someone in history who had that same thought and is in the room backing you up. That's what a citation does. You may be starting out, but if a respected voice is behind your claim, the "I'm a beginner" label matters less. The work speaks.

The inverse is also true. If you're making a claim and genuinely can't find anyone in history who has said something similar, that's a signal. Either you've found something genuinely new — or you've drifted. Let existing work be your compass. It shows you the direction. It doesn't tell you what you actually see.

Don't try to win the argument with your own voice. Bring five people — living or dead — into the room to argue for you.

ii.3 — Find Your Strongest Opponent

Before you commit to an idea, do this:

Find the one person — living or dead — whose thinking most directly challenges what you're trying to say. Not someone who disagrees on a minor point. The strongest possible opposition. The person whose worldview, if correct, makes your idea wrong or unnecessary.

Then sit with that honestly. Compare the worldview their idea came from with the worldview yours comes from. What did they have access to that you don't? What do you have access to that they didn't? Where does your context differ from theirs in ways that matter to the argument?

This does one of two things. Either you find where your idea is actually weak — a constraint you hadn't accounted for, an assumption that doesn't hold — and you fix it before someone else finds it for you. Or you find exactly how to extend their work: "she disagreed there, but here's why that disagreement doesn't apply in this context, and here's the evidence."

Either outcome is useful. Both make the work stronger.

The point is not to be contrarian or to hunt for fights. The point is that an idea that has survived its strongest opposition is an idea you can actually stand behind.

Module iii — The Practical Layer

No philosophy here. Just what you actually do, with examples of what it looks like in practice. These came from real questions and real friction. If something reads like it's very specific, that's because it is.

iii.1 — Before You Start: The Basics You Actually Need

The most common question from people who want to get started: how much do I need to know before I'm ready?

Here's the honest answer: less than you think, and the readiness doesn't come before you start — it comes from starting.

But there are a few things that genuinely help to have in place, because not having them will slow you down in ways that feel frustrating rather than productive.

Reading papers

If you've never read an academic paper, read one this week. It will feel dense and strange. Read it anyway. Look up every term you don't know. Then read another. The discomfort fades faster than you think — usually within four to six weeks of reading regularly, you start to feel the structure, recognise the moves, know where to look for the actual claim versus the context-setting. There's no shortcut here. You just have to log the hours.

A good starting move: find a paper in an area you're already interested in. Not the most important paper in the field — just a recent one that looks accessible. Read the abstract, then the conclusion, then the whole thing. Do that twice a week for a month.

Writing clearly

You don't need to write beautifully. You need to write clearly. If someone reads your sentence and has to work to figure out what you meant, rewrite it. Read your own work out loud. If you stumble over something, it needs to be rewritten.

Google Scholar and Zotero

Know how to search Scholar. Know how to use filters (date range, number of citations). Set up a free Zotero account and start saving papers to it — this becomes essential once you're working across more than a handful of sources. You do not want to reconstruct where you found something at 11pm before a deadline.

Basic folder discipline

Sounds trivial. It isn't. Decide on a folder structure before you start a project and stick to it. Keep versions. Label files with dates. final_FINAL_v3_actual.pdf is a joke that becomes real very quickly if you don't have a system.

How long until it feels comfortable?

Genuinely — two to three months of regular reading and one small project where you actually try to write something up. Not comfortable as in easy. Comfortable as in you know the shape of the discomfort and it doesn't feel like evidence that you shouldn't be there.

iii.2 — Sleep, Rest, and the All-Nighter

This is in the practical module because it's a practical thing, not a wellness platitude.

Try to get at least six hours of sleep. Not because it sounds responsible — because the quality of thinking you do on less than that is genuinely worse, and you often can't tell while it's happening. Decisions that feel obvious and correct at 2am look different in the morning.

It is extremely common when you're absorbed in a problem to look up and realise it's midnight and you haven't eaten since noon. That absorption is real and it's part of what makes research feel alive. It's also how you end up running yourself down in ways that take longer to recover from than the all-nighter was worth.

More useful than pushing through: rest, and start fresh. A genuinely rested two hours will produce better thinking than a depleted six. This is not an excuse to be lazy. It's an instruction to be strategic about when and how you work.

If you're a night person, work at night — but protect the sleep. Shift the sleep window, don't shrink it.

And if you're consistently not sleeping, consistently running on empty, consistently feeling like you're behind — that's a conversation to have with whoever you're working with, before it becomes a problem that affects the work. Say something early. A clear "I don't have the bandwidth this week" is more useful to everyone than a slow collapse that no one saw coming.

iii.3 — How Ideas Actually Come

The honest answer is: usually not from sitting down to look for ideas.

The pattern that comes up again and again — at least in my experience — is observation. You notice something. It bothers you or interests you or doesn't make sense given something else you know. You pull on that thread.

Some real examples:

Example 01

A blog post by a researcher included a video of a robot cleaning up broken glass. Watching it, the question came up: why is this the dominant way we think about robots — as things designed to approximate human movements and tasks? Is there a whole design space we're ignoring by starting from the human blueprint? The literature check showed a lot of scattered projects that aligned with the idea but no single term or synthesis that made it canonical. That felt like something worth writing. That became Beyond the Human Blueprint: Toward Robomorphic Design in Autonomous Systems.

Example 02

At a hackathon, a computer declared Maggi instant noodles healthier than plain rice. The immediate question: why doesn't the system understand food the way people who eat it understand food? Why isn't there a better infrastructure for this? That friction — between how food is classified in databases and how food actually exists in Indian kitchens — became Indian Food Informatics Data 2026.

Example 03

Building a first web app, web accessibility wasn't part of the plan until near the end, when it became obvious it should have been there from the start. The question: why isn't accessibility taught as a default the same way we teach type=email in an HTML form? That became the web accessibility series.

The pattern across all of these is not sitting down with a list of research gaps and methodically searching for an opening. It's noticing friction. Noticing something that's normal but maybe shouldn't be, or that doesn't add up given something else you know. The question that comes out of that observation — why is it this way, and does it have to be? — is usually closer to a real research question than anything you'll find by searching for gaps in the literature.

So if you're trying to find your first idea: observe. Look at the things you already encounter and interact with. Look for the friction you've gotten used to. Look for the thing that seems obvious but when you actually try to explain why it's done that way, the answer gets complicated.

You don't need to be in a lab to do this. You need to be paying attention.

iii.4 — How to Ask a Question

There's a version of asking a question that wastes everyone's time including yours, and there's a version that actually moves things forward. The difference is in how much work you've done before you send the message.

Before you ask anyone anything

First, look it up. Google it. Ask an AI model to explain it. Try a different AI if the first one doesn't make it clear. Most of the time — not all of the time, but most — the answer exists somewhere and finding it yourself builds something that being told doesn't.

Second, try to locate exactly where the confusion is. Not "I don't understand this paper" — but which line, which term, which specific claim is the one you're stuck on.

The difference in practice

Question about FSSAI and ITC-HS
✗

"I don't understand what FSSAI is and how it relates to ITC-HS codes." — This is a search query, not a question for a researcher. Google this. It will answer it.

✓

"I've read the FSSAI definition of 'health supplement' from the 2020 regulations and the ITC-HS classification for the same product category. They seem to define the boundary differently — FSSAI draws it based on nutritional function, ITC-HS draws it based on form and ingredient. I think this means a product could legitimately fall into different categories under each system, which would create a compliance gap. Is my reading of that contradiction right, or am I missing something about how the two systems are meant to interact?"

Question about a document
✗

"What does this paragraph mean?"

✓

"I'm trying to understand this line from Section 3.2 of the document you shared: [paste the line]. I think it means X, but that seems to contradict what the earlier section said about Y. Am I misreading it?"

The format to remember: here's what I found, here's what I think it means, here's where I'm uncertain — is my reading right? That second version tells whoever you're asking exactly what you've already understood, where you've gotten to, and what specifically you need. And half the time, writing it out that precisely is enough to answer it yourself.

iii.5 — How to Say No (And How to Say Yes)

A half-hearted yes is worse than a clear no. This is not a motivational statement — it's a practical one. When you say yes to something you can't actually do, you create a problem that compounds. The person who asked you is planning around your yes. When it doesn't materialise, or materialises at half-capacity, they've lost time they could have used differently.

A clear no, given early, lets everyone adjust.

How to say no to a PI or lead researcher

The instinct is to apologise a lot and be vague about the reason. Don't. Be specific and be early.

PI sends a task on Monday with a Friday deadline
✗

"Hi, I'm so sorry, I've been really busy and I don't think I'll be able to finish this in time, I'm really sorry about that, I'll try my best but I can't promise anything."

✓

"Hi — I want to flag early that I won't be able to get this to you by Friday. I have [specific commitment] through Wednesday and realistically can't give this the attention it needs before then. Can we move the deadline to Monday? I'll have it to you by end of day."

✓

"I want to be upfront — I don't have the bandwidth for this right now. I'd rather say that clearly now than take it on and not do it properly. Is there someone else who could pick this up, or can it wait until [timeframe]?"

That's it. No performance of guilt. No vague promises. Just the actual situation, early enough to be useful.

How to push back on feedback

When you get feedback you disagree with, the goal is not to win. The goal is to make sure the right decision gets made, which sometimes means changing your mind and sometimes means making a case for why you think your original approach was right.

Reviewer says a section needs to be cut. You think it's necessary.
✗

"I actually think this section is important and should stay in."

✓

"I want to make the case for keeping this section, but I want to make sure I'm understanding your concern first — is it about length, or do you think the content itself isn't adding to the argument? If it's length, I think I can tighten it significantly. If you think the content isn't pulling its weight, I'd want to understand that more before deciding — because I included it specifically to address [X], and I'm not sure where else that gets covered. Open to being wrong about this, just want to make sure we're not losing something that matters."

How to flag you're stuck or need more time

Do this early. Seriously. Not the night before something is due.

You've hit a wall
✓

"I want to flag that I've hit a wall on [specific thing]. I've tried [what you've tried] and I'm not sure how to move forward. I don't want to get to Friday and have nothing. Can we talk through it, or can you point me somewhere specific?"

It's a time issue
✓

"I realised today that I underestimated how long [specific part] was going to take. I want to be upfront about that now rather than scramble at the end. Here's where I am: [actual status]. I think I can have it done by [realistic date]. Does that work, or do we need to talk about scope?"

The consistent principle across all of this: say the real thing, say it early, say it specifically. The instinct to soften and delay makes things worse almost every time.

iii.6 — How to Red-Team Your Own Idea

Red-teaming means trying to break your own idea before someone else does. It's not about being harsh on yourself — it's about doing the honest work of checking whether what you're saying actually holds up.

Step 1: Write the claim as a single clear sentence

Not a paragraph. One sentence. If you can't do this, the idea isn't clear enough yet to defend.

"The current FSSAI classification framework creates systematic ambiguity for products that qualify as both a food and a health supplement under existing definitions."

Step 2: Find the strongest thing that could make this wrong

Ask: what would need to be true for this claim to be false? Is there a definition somewhere that resolves the ambiguity I think exists? Is there a regulatory mechanism that handles this that I haven't found yet? Who would most confidently disagree with me, and what would their argument be?

Go look for that. Actually look. Not "I can imagine someone might say X" but "let me find whether anyone has actually said X and what their evidence was."

Step 3: List where it breaks

Be honest. If your claim only holds for certain product categories and not others, write that down. If it holds in one regulatory context but not another, write that down. These aren't weaknesses to hide — they're the scope of your actual claim. A precise claim with clear limits is more useful and more credible than a broad claim with hidden holes.

Step 4: Decide what survives

After all of that, what's still standing? That's your actual argument. It might be smaller than what you started with. That's fine. A smaller true thing is better than a larger uncertain one.

Using AI for red-teaming

This is actually one of the better uses of AI in research. Paste your claim and ask it to steelman the opposing view. Ask it what the strongest counterargument is. Ask it what assumptions you're making that might not hold. It will sometimes give you things that are wrong or irrelevant — you'll know which ones those are because you've done the reading. But occasionally it will surface an angle you hadn't considered, and that's worth having.

What you should not do: ask it to write the section, then move on. The red-teaming only works if you're the one actually sitting with the discomfort of whether the idea holds up.

iii.7 — Using AI Right

Use it. Just know what it is and isn't good for.

Where it genuinely helps

Finding documents. If you describe what you're looking for — a government regulation from a specific year, a definition from a particular standards body, papers in a specific area — AI is faster than search for narrowing down where to look. Then you go get the actual document and read it yourself.

Explaining a concept differently. If you've read something three times and it isn't landing, ask an AI to explain it from a different angle. Sometimes a different framing is all you need.

Red-teaming. See above. Asking it what's wrong with your argument, what you might be missing, what the strongest counter is.

Checking if a sentence is clear. Paste a sentence and ask if it's saying what you mean. Useful gut-check. Not a substitute for your own judgement.

Where it will quietly replace your thinking if you let it

Writing your argument for you. The thinking that happens when you have to find the words for an idea is the research. If you outsource that to an AI, you're outsourcing the part that matters. The output might look fine. It won't have been built on actual understanding.

Neutral wording. AI has a deep habit of adding jargon to sound balanced, which is not the same thing as being neutral. It produces sentences that sound like they were written to sound neutral rather than sentences that actually are. Use the two-sides test instead (see ii.1). Trust your own read.

Telling you what sources to cite. It will hallucinate. Confidently, specifically, incorrectly. Always go get the actual source and read it.

The principle: use AI to find things faster and to pressure-test your thinking. Don't use it to do the thinking. The thinking is where the research happens, and you are doing fine.

iii.8 — Your Name on Your Work

Every document you send out — every report, every draft, every preprint — should have:

  • Your full name
  • Your email address
  • Your ORCID

ORCID is a persistent identifier for researchers. It's free, it takes ten minutes to set up, and it follows you across institutions, name changes, affiliations. Go to orcid.org and create one now if you don't have one. Share the ID with whoever you're working with.

Your intellectual property is yours. The lab or institution you work with is the context that makes certain work possible — it's not the owner of your thinking. Make sure your name is actually on your work, linked in a way that persists and can be found.

This is administrative. It also matters. Do it.

This guide is added to when real questions make new lessons necessary. If something isn't here and you think it should be, email lalithaar.research@gmail.com.

Take care of yourself first. Everything else follows from that.
